jokes.text: Why do we have to have a party every time someone in this family is released from prison? - Tradition.
jokes.setup: Normal families celebrate graduations or achievements
jokes.punchline: The Bluth family's tradition is prison releases
jokes.explanation: Perfect character beat that establishes the family's criminal dysfunction as casual tradition. Economy and character integration are excellent.

jokes.text: Like that god-awful thing you used to make me do on your birthday. Cook?
jokes.setup: Expectation of something sexual or inappropriate
jokes.punchline: It was just cooking, revealing Lucille's entitled laziness
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection that perfectly captures Lucille's character - the idea that cooking would be traumatic to her.

jokes.text: Not in front of the grandkids. I don't want them to know about the shady business scams - We lied to investors, so we have no product and we're two million dollars in debt.
jokes.setup: Lucille wants to protect the children from family secrets
jokes.punchline: Lindsay immediately blurts out all the illegal details
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Lindsay's obliviousness, though the structure is fairly predictable.

jokes.text: Oh, don't be ridiculous. My name isn't on anything.
jokes.setup: Family faces criminal liability
jokes.punchline: Lucille's only concern is her own legal protection
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille moment - showing her complete self-interest and criminal savvy disguised as casual conversation.

jokes.text: Know what? I've never been attracted to handsome men. Ah.
jokes.setup: Rebel reassures George Michael about competition
jokes.punchline: George Michael's deflated 'Ah' reaction to being called not handsome
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective reaction beat. The 'Ah' perfectly captures George Michael's realization.

jokes.text: coding and, um... also programming
jokes.setup: George Michael tries to sound tech-savvy
jokes.punchline: He uses coding and programming as if they're different things
jokes.explanation: Shows George Michael's inexperience with tech terminology in a believable way. Small but character-appropriate.

jokes.text: So stop having feelings for her. What? Is that something you can do with people?
jokes.setup: Michael casually suggests turning off emotions
jokes.punchline: George Michael's innocent confusion about emotional detachment
jokes.explanation: Nice contrast between Michael's cynicism and George Michael's emotional sincerity.

jokes.text: Yeah, once I learned how to do it with my parents, it was easy with everyone else. It's like a heart switch, you know? Click. I love you. Click. I love you not.
jokes.setup: Michael explains emotional detachment
jokes.punchline: He demonstrates with disturbing casual ease, including the 'click' sound effects
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment showing Michael's emotional damage through dark comedy. The 'click' sound effect is perfect.

jokes.text: No, but in my defense, I'm not a sociopath. Click.
jokes.setup: George Michael explains why he can't turn off emotions
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately demonstrates the sociopathic behavior with another 'click'
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate callback that proves George Michael's point about Michael being a sociopath.

jokes.text: Gob still had a very uncomfortable 'heart on' for his friend Tony
jokes.setup: Narrator describes Gob's feelings
jokes.punchline: 'Heart on' as wordplay for emotional attachment vs sexual arousal
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that works on multiple levels and fits the show's style of narrator commentary.

jokes.text: Oh, who knows what makes a guy think he can pass for straight? I mean, if he really is gay, he can try to hide it, but trust me, it will come out in the most unexpected balls.
jokes.setup: Gob pontificates about sexuality
jokes.punchline: Says 'balls' instead of 'ways' while being completely oblivious to the irony
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob malapropism that's both accidentally sexual and ironically oblivious given his own situation.

jokes.text: Uh... Excuse me, 'balls'? You realize you just said 'balls'? - But you said balls. - Oh, well that's three times I'm hearing balls now.
jokes.setup: George Michael points out Gob's verbal slip
jokes.punchline: The conversation becomes entirely about counting how many times they've said 'balls'
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation comedy but feels somewhat forced. The counting becomes the joke itself.

jokes.text: That's the sign of a healthy business. It's closed in the middle of a Tuesday, and the owner's locked out.
jokes.setup: George Michael suggests the business being locked is good
jokes.punchline: Michael points out this is actually terrible business practice
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic observation about business incompetence. Michael as the voice of reason.

jokes.text: Of course, these are paid protestors that the magicians hired. Not real protestors, but that's kind of the norm now, isn't it?
jokes.setup: Narrator explains the protesters are fake
jokes.punchline: Casual commentary on real-world political theater
jokes.explanation: Timely political commentary but feels somewhat detached from the episode's main comedy.

jokes.text: David, you want Pop-Tarts for dinner? Mom, don't say my name!
jokes.setup: Anonymous hackers are supposed to be mysterious and threatening
jokes.punchline: One is interrupted by his mom offering Pop-Tarts for dinner
jokes.explanation: Excellent deflation of hacker mystique. The mundane family interaction undermines their threatening image perfectly.

jokes.text: You bet your balls I can get you some Guy Fawkeses. Don't hold your balls. I would not hold my balls on that one.
jokes.setup: George Michael asks if Gob can get Guy Fawkes masks
jokes.punchline: Gob continues his unconscious 'balls' fixation while mixing up idioms
jokes.explanation: Continues the running gag effectively but starts to feel repetitive. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Oh, speaking of maniacs, let's watch this again.
jokes.setup: Family discusses serious criminal situation
jokes.punchline: Lucille treats it as entertainment to rewatch
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille moment showing her detachment from consequences and treating family drama as soap opera.

jokes.text: Well, it's not you!
jokes.setup: Someone asks if the person in the video is Oscar
jokes.punchline: Lucille's immediate certainty that it's not George
jokes.explanation: Quick character beat showing Lucille's knowledge of her husband's cowardice/incompetence.

jokes.text: Buster's in jail? My boy. My boy.
jokes.setup: Oscar learns Buster is in jail
jokes.punchline: His melodramatic reaction to news about his biological son
jokes.explanation: Works on rewatch knowing Oscar is Buster's biological father. Mild melodrama.

jokes.text: He's got a lot of spunk. I like that on a guy.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Buster's prison break
jokes.punchline: Lucille's inappropriately sexual comment about her son
jokes.explanation: Classic inappropriate Lucille comment with sexual undertones about her son. Well-executed character beat.

jokes.text: I'll go with you, and we will find our son together, and perhaps we will find each other along the way.
jokes.setup: Crisis situation requires action
jokes.punchline: George turns it into cheesy romantic movie dialogue
jokes.explanation: Shows George's tendency toward melodrama and inappropriate timing. Decent character work.

jokes.text: Just as I found my son, I lost him. And just as I lost my wife, I... I found my replacement wife, but then I... I lost her. I... I have nobody. Well, you got me, Dad. Not now, Maeby. I have... what? Like, one person. Click.
jokes.setup: George Sr. melodramatically lists his losses
jokes.punchline: He dismisses Maeby mid-speech, then immediately uses Michael's 'click' heart switch
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation and callback. The 'click' at the end perfectly caps the cruelty to Maeby.

jokes.text: Well, you got me, Dad. Not now, Maeby. I have... what? Like, one person. Click.
jokes.setup: George Sr. lamenting that he has nobody
jokes.punchline: Dismisses Maeby who just offered support, then uses Michael's 'click'
jokes.explanation: Great callback that shows George Sr. adopting Michael's emotional detachment. The dismissal of Maeby makes his loneliness ironic.

jokes.text: We forgot the debt. Basically, what we did was we absorbed everything, including you.
jokes.setup: Discussion of business debt forgiveness
jokes.punchline: They 'forgot' the debt (didn't forgive it) and 'absorbed' George like a company asset
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay about business terminology. The absurdity of absorbing a person works well.

jokes.text: Gob doesn't know that there's a B in the word 'debt.'
jokes.setup: Question about Gob's business knowledge
jokes.punchline: Gob doesn't understand basic spelling/pronunciation
jokes.explanation: Perfect encapsulation of Gob's stupidity in one economical line. Highly quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: It takes a lot out of me, but they like to look at me as the sticky white glue that holds the family together.
jokes.setup: Tobias describes his family role
jokes.punchline: Accidentally sexual metaphor about being 'sticky white glue'
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias accidental double entendre. The sexual innuendo is perfectly in character and oblivious.

jokes.text: Roof balls! Jesus, quiet.
jokes.setup: Tobias hits his head on the attic roof
jokes.punchline: Instead of cursing properly, he says 'roof balls'
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy combined with Tobias's tendency toward malapropisms. The invented curse word works.

jokes.text: Oh, this isn't a real house. This is a model house. How long do we have to stay here? Just until I can support us on my actor salary. Oh... Quiet. You can be excited, but quietly.
jokes.setup: Family asks about living situation duration
jokes.punchline: Tobias's delusional belief he'll make money acting, plus misreading groans as excitement
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Tobias's delusions about his career and misreading social cues.

jokes.text: I guess stuff does happen like this in real life.
jokes.setup: Oscar compares their situation to silent movie comedy
jokes.punchline: Realization that their absurd life actually resembles slapstick comedy
jokes.explanation: Nice meta moment acknowledging the show's own comedic style through character observation.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. It's a fake hand. How can it hurt? No, it's a rental from prison, and Mother said my next hand... is gonna be out of my own pocket
jokes.setup: Oscar questions why fake hand can hurt
jokes.punchline: Buster's literal interpretation of 'out of pocket' for hand replacement
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay that's both literal and punny. Shows Buster's literal-mindedness and family dysfunction.

jokes.text: What are you doing with the hose? Wha... No, I didn't know it was on. I mean, I would never purposefully run a car with the garage door closed with the family in crisis.
jokes.setup: Michael finds George with what appears to be a suicide attempt
jokes.punchline: George's bumbling denial reveals he was actually attempting suicide
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy handled well. George's denial actually confirms the suicide attempt in classic AD style.

jokes.text: Okay. You know, Dad, this is an electric car. Oh. So, the only thing you're killing is my battery.
jokes.setup: Apparent carbon monoxide suicide attempt
jokes.punchline: Electric car makes it impossible - just draining battery instead
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection that deflates the dark moment perfectly. Modern technology thwarting old-school suicide method is very funny.

jokes.text: K means thousand. Can you believe Adhir just expected me to know that? I must've told him K times, 'You gotta help me with these things.'
jokes.setup: Gob explains what K means in financial terms
jokes.punchline: Uses 'K times' while complaining about not knowing what K means
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay showing Gob's obliviousness. He uses the term correctly while claiming ignorance.

jokes.text: I used to train dogs, so I'm very patient. - Good Gob. Good K.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining his patience with Gob
jokes.punchline: Treats Gob like a dog he's training, uses 'K' like a dog command
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback combining dog training with the K joke - economical and character-specific

jokes.text: I used to train dogs, so I'm very patient. And $10,000 for a printer? - 10K. - Good Gob. Good K.
jokes.setup: Michael reviews expenses, Gob corrects terminology
jokes.punchline: Michael treats Gob like a trained dog with 'Good Gob. Good K.'
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback combining Michael's dog training comment with Gob's K lesson. Condescending but effective.

jokes.text: Hey, you want to spend a couple hundred bucks on a 3-D receipt, I'll have it printed and in your mouth before sunset.
jokes.setup: Michael asks for receipt of 3-D printer
jokes.punchline: Gob offers to 3-D print a receipt and put it in Michael's mouth
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation typical of Gob's overconfident nonsense. The 'in your mouth' part is unexpectedly specific.

jokes.text: The Austero books are for British eyes only, as they say. ♪ For British eyes only ♪
jokes.setup: Accountant explains restricted access to books
jokes.punchline: Reference to James Bond theme with unnecessary musical flourish
jokes.explanation: Decent pop culture reference with musical element. Works but not especially clever or character-specific.

jokes.text: There's only room at this company for one copresident. What's next? You're gonna want one of my parking spaces, Michael?
jokes.setup: Gob asserts his business authority
jokes.punchline: Worries about sharing parking spaces while being 'copresident'
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Gob's petty concerns and misunderstanding of 'co-' prefix.

jokes.text: Oh, well, Michael, don't worry, 'cause I already went there. ♪ Balls in the air... ♪
jokes.setup: Michael mentions 'trump' in context
jokes.punchline: Gob misunderstands and references his juggling act with balls theme song
jokes.explanation: Continues the balls theme with Gob's magic act. Somewhat forced but character-consistent.

jokes.text: Would you be terribly offended if I offered you a candy? - I don't think Dad wanted me to see this. - I don't, either.
jokes.setup: Accountant nervously offering candy during financial revelation
jokes.punchline: Admits he also thinks Michael shouldn't see the books
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of discomfort - accountant's honesty making situation worse

jokes.text: We got to face facts. They're stuck. These stick. They're stickers...
jokes.setup: Michael tries to make a serious point about being stuck
jokes.punchline: Gets literally stuck to something while talking about being stuck
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy that literalizes the metaphor. The escalation of stuck/stick/stickers works.

jokes.text: Maybe even burning the... well, midweek oil. I quit. I shouldn't have called it the easy choice.
jokes.setup: Michael talks about working hard, mentions 'midweek oil'
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately quits after Michael says that's the hard choice
jokes.explanation: Good malapropism (midnight oil) and immediate reversal when Gob realizes he chose wrong.

jokes.text: Outside, inside, outside, inside... Now, we cannot draw attention to ourselves. - I'm just a guy fixing a broken hand. - Right. And I'm... I'm just a guy who's wearing a blue hat. Nothing to see here, folks. Nothing to see.
jokes.setup: They try to blend in inconspicuously
jokes.punchline: Their 'stealth' involves loudly announcing what they're doing and saying 'nothing to see here'
jokes.explanation: Classic 'trying not to be suspicious while being extremely suspicious' comedy. Well-executed but familiar.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm like a kid in a candy shop. - No way. - I think I saw William Shatner wearing this while riding a bicycle once.
jokes.setup: Buster excited about prosthetic hands selection
jokes.punchline: Random specific William Shatner reference for prosthetic
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist specificity typical of the show's random pop culture references

jokes.text: Oh, I'm like a kid in a candy shop. No way. I think I saw William Shatner wearing this while riding a bicycle once.
jokes.setup: They find the prosthetics lab
jokes.punchline: Buster's excitement and oddly specific William Shatner reference
jokes.explanation: Good Buster moment with his childlike excitement and random specific reference. Character-appropriate.

jokes.text: It sort of reminds me of Mother's hand, except it's less veiny, and warmer.
jokes.setup: Buster examines a prosthetic hand
jokes.punchline: Inappropriately intimate comparison to his mother's hand
jokes.explanation: Perfect Buster moment showing his disturbing relationship with his mother. The details make it worse.

jokes.text: This is just as tough for you as it is for me. Tougher, maybe. Man without a king.
jokes.setup: Gob prepares to quit his job
jokes.punchline: Treats quitting like a tragic heroic moment with grandiose language
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Gob's dramatic self-importance and grandiosity over mundane events.

jokes.text: You do know that Michael didn't fire you, you quit. - As any proud gay man would do in the face of such bigotry.
jokes.setup: Accountant correcting Gob's version of events
jokes.punchline: Gob doubles down, framing his quit as anti-gay discrimination
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Gob's ability to reframe any situation to his advantage

jokes.text: Probably for being gay, too. You do know that Michael didn't fire you, you quit. As any proud gay man would do in the face of such bigotry.
jokes.setup: Gob claims persecution for being gay
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts the correction by doubling down on the persecution narrative
jokes.explanation: Shows Gob's commitment to his narrative despite facts. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: You should've seen the look on his face when I do my impression of you. You know... What's the problem with Apu?
jokes.setup: Gob mentions doing an impression
jokes.punchline: The accountant's deadpan response to obvious racism
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable but character-appropriate racism from Gob, with good straight-man response.

jokes.text: Oh, you're just mad 'cause you can't have a hamburger.
jokes.setup: Accountant explains conversion therapy doesn't work
jokes.punchline: Gob reduces it to religious dietary restrictions
jokes.explanation: Shows Gob's ignorance and stereotyping. Character-consistent but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: I didn't understand a word you just said, little guy.
jokes.setup: Accountant gives thoughtful explanation about conversion therapy
jokes.punchline: Gob dismisses it with casual racism and ignorance
jokes.explanation: Consistent with Gob's character but relies heavily on cringe without much craft. The racism is uncomfortable.

jokes.text: You know Betty White has this same car? - You know Betty White? - We share a weed dealer.
jokes.setup: Casual mention of Betty White having same car
jokes.punchline: Reveals unexpectedly specific connection through drug dealer
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect economy. Betty White drug dealer joke is unexpected and specific.

jokes.text: I don't mean the alkies, I mean the, uh, the guys who wear the creepy masks, hang out online. The Guy Fawkeses.
jokes.setup: George Michael explains Anonymous hackers
jokes.punchline: Calls them 'alkies' (alcoholics) before correcting to 'Guy Fawkeses'
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing George Michael's nervousness and confusion. The alkies mistake is believable.

jokes.text: You're taking me as a hostage in case the cops see us? Hey, guy, I wouldn't know about that. - We can pretend we're cousins. - Cousins?
jokes.setup: Stan assumes he's being taken hostage
jokes.punchline: Oscar's casual 'I wouldn't know about that' followed by cousin suggestion
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the absurd situation. Oscar's casual response to hostage accusation works well.

jokes.text: You hired a bunch of foxy guys to protest me because I said I hated hot guys.
jokes.setup: Rebel sees the attractive protesters
jokes.punchline: Misinterprets George Michael's scheme as revenge for her comment about handsome men
jokes.explanation: Good callback and misdirection. Rebel's interpretation makes sense given her earlier comment.

jokes.text: Yeah. Oh, my goodness. Fakeblock is so impressive. - Yeah. - I mean, Fakeblock's incredible. - Fakeblock is... - Yeah, Fakeblock is real. It's my place.
jokes.setup: George Michael expects to show empty fake office
jokes.punchline: Discovers real, impressive tech company actually exists there
jokes.explanation: Excellent plot twist that completely reverses expectations. The repetition of 'Fakeblock' builds the reveal perfectly.

jokes.text: Yeah. Oh, my goodness. Fakeblock is so impressive. - Yeah. - I mean, Fakeblock's incredible. - Fakeblock is... - Yeah, Fakeblock is real. It's my place.
jokes.setup: George Michael expecting to see empty space to prove Fakeblock is fake
jokes.punchline: Real tech company is operating there, Maeby reveals it's actually hers
jokes.explanation: Major plot reversal with perfect setup. George Michael's fake company is real, but belongs to someone else. Excellent misdirection.

jokes.text: You took me for Chinese. The Italian place was the first time I caught you with your secretary, and you asked, 'How did you ever find me?' ♪ How did you ever find me? ♪
jokes.setup: George tries romantic memory of first date
jokes.punchline: Lucille corrects it to memory of catching him cheating, complete with song
jokes.explanation: Excellent reversal that shows the marriage's dysfunction. The musical element adds perfect absurdity.

jokes.text: I want you, Lucille. In what way, George? Sexually? Yes. Sexual-ish-ly.
jokes.setup: George declares he wants Lucille
jokes.punchline: His hedging on 'sexually' to 'sexual-ish-ly'
jokes.explanation: Perfect George Sr. moment showing his sexual dysfunction and verbal awkwardness. 'Sexual-ish-ly' is perfectly pathetic.

jokes.text: Look, here's the deal. - I can't... get hard. I... - Live without me. It's really getting hard, uh, to live without you.
jokes.setup: George starts to confess erectile dysfunction
jokes.punchline: Lucille finishes his sentence differently, George plays into the wordplay
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay around 'hard' that works on multiple levels. Well-structured misdirection.

jokes.text: He didn't say a word until we fired the wet nurse. He didn't stop talking about that for three years. - Until we caved.
jokes.setup: Nostalgic memory of Buster starting to talk
jokes.punchline: Reveals he was traumatized by firing the wet nurse and they had to rehire her
jokes.explanation: Good character moment revealing family dysfunction and Buster's psychological issues. Fits the family dynamic perfectly.

jokes.text: Only because Michael drives an electric car. Trust me, the whole 'trying to kill yourself' thing, turn off.
jokes.setup: George claims he's alive
jokes.punchline: Lucille references the failed suicide attempt and calls it unsexy
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback with perfect Lucille bluntness. Treating suicide attempts as a dating turnoff is peak Lucille.

jokes.text: I don't call it that, I call it 'a cry for help,' but I think help has just arrived and it's saying, 'Cry for fun!'
jokes.setup: George responds to criticism of suicide attempt
jokes.punchline: Transforms 'cry for help' into 'cry for fun' with forced optimism
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay showing George's desperate attempt to reframe his situation positively. Character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Hurt people, hurt people.
jokes.setup: George reflects on the conversation
jokes.punchline: Delivers pseudo-profound statement that could mean two different things
jokes.explanation: Nicely ambiguous statement that works as both psychology insight and threat. Good economy.
